facility_name: GRC ICEtank C3 One — US Air Force Hill AFB (Immersion Cooled)
operator_company: US Air Force / GRC (Green Revolution Cooling)
parent_company: US Department of Defense — US Air Force
full_address: Hill Air Force Base, Ogden, UT 84056
facility_type: Military / Modular Containerized Data Center
customer_type_if_known: US Air Force — tactical and operational computing
build_phase_if_known: Deployed ~2016; 3+ years cumulative testing with 100% uptime reported
cooling_type: Single-phase liquid immersion cooling — GRC ICEtank containerized system with cooling tower heat rejection. ElectroSafe dielectric coolant. 2-4 purpose-built racks per container. 93.1% reduction in cooling energy. 18.1% less server power. 41.4% total power reduction.
opportunity_summary: US Air Force deployed GRC ICEtank immersion-cooled containerized data centers at Hill AFB. C3 One variant uses cooling tower for heat rejection. Tested cumulatively for 3+ years with 100% uptime. PUE of 1.05. 93.1% reduction in cooling PUE. Turnkey system deployable in under 24 hours. GRC working with USAF on next-gen production units. Strong proof-of-concept for military immersion cooling at scale.
confidence_reason: GRC press release (BusinessWire Feb 2019), DCD article, HPCwire confirm deployment at Hill AFB. GRC customer page confirms specs. USAF confirmed partner.
source_1_title: GRC Designs Fully-integrated, Containerized Liquid Immersion Cooling Solutions for the US Air Force
source_1_evidence_snippet: GRC deployed modular immersion-cooled data centers at Hill AFB and Tinker AFB. C3 One (Hill) uses cooling tower. C3 Two (Tinker) uses dry cooler. 2-4 racks per container. ElectroSafe coolant. 3 years cumulative testing, 100% uptime.
source_2_title: GRC details immersion cooling deployment for the US Air Force
source_2_evidence_snippet: GRC ICEtank at Hill AFB and Tinker AFB. 93.1% cooling PUE reduction. 18.1% less server power. 41.4% total power reduction. Deployable in under 24 hours.
notes: VERIFIED MILITARY IMMERSION COOLING DEPLOYMENT. C3 One at Hill AFB is one of the first confirmed US military immersion-cooled data center deployments. GRC backed by NSF and DoD. Next-gen production units in development with USAF.
expansion_details: GRC working with USAF to develop next-gen production units
pipeline_evidence_snippet: Deployed at Hill AFB ~2016. 3+ years tested with 100% uptime. Next-gen units planned.

facility_name: NSA Laboratory for Physical Sciences — GRC Immersion Cooling (ICEraQ)
parent_company: US Department of Defense — NSA
full_address: NSA Laboratory for Physical Sciences, College Park, MD
facility_type: Intelligence Agency / Research Lab Data Center
customer_type_if_known: NSA Advanced Computing Research team
build_phase_if_known: Customer since 2012; GRC ICEraQ immersion cooling system installed and evaluated
cooling_type: Single-phase liquid immersion cooling — GRC ICEraQ system. ElectroSafe dielectric coolant. Up to 100kW per rack. Eliminates chillers, CRAC units, raised flooring, temperature/humidity controls. 95% cooling energy cost savings. Up to 60% CapEx reduction vs air-cooled.
opportunity_summary: NSA's Advanced Computing Research team at the Laboratory for Physical Sciences has been a GRC immersion cooling customer since 2012. Achieved PUE of 1.05. 100kW per rack (vs 10-15kW with air). 95% cooling energy cost savings. NSA engineer David Prucnal confirmed substantial CapEx reduction. NSA stated potential to cut future data center construction costs in half. Rapid global deployment capability highlighted.
confidence_reason: GRC official customer success page names NSA. DataCenterKnowledge article (2013) confirms NSA testing. NSA engineer quoted. Exact facility location within Lab for Physical Sciences inferred but not disclosed.
source_1_title: National Security Agency — GRC Customer Success
source_1_evidence_snippet: NSA customer since 2012. ICEraQ system. PUE 1.05. Up to 100kW per rack. 95% cooling energy savings. 60% CapEx reduction. Quote from David Prucnal, P.E. at NSA.
source_2_title: NSA Exploring Use of Mineral Oil to Cool its Servers
source_2_evidence_snippet: NSA Lab for Physical Sciences acquired GRC oil-immersion cooling in 2012. 30kW per rack vs 10-15kW air. Potential to cut construction costs in half. 250-gallon CarnotJet racks.
notes: INTELLIGENCE AGENCY IMMERSION COOLING. One of earliest known US government immersion cooling deployments. NSA chose immersion to increase rack density and reduce costs. Exact MW and rack count classified/undisclosed.
pipeline_evidence_snippet: NSA customer since 2012. Ongoing immersion cooling deployment.

Feb 2025 Army Corps environmental assessment filed — potential campus expansion. NSA tested GRC immersion cooling elsewhere (Lab for Physical Sciences) — possible future retrofit candidate.
opportunity_summary: Massive NSA facility using 1.7M gal water/day for evaporative cooling in water-stressed Utah. 65MW constant power. 4 data halls at 18.75MW critical each. $1.7B facility. Feb 2025 Army Corps environmental assessment filed for potential campus expansion. NSA already proven immersion cooling at Lab for Physical Sciences (PUE 1.05) — strong candidate for immersion cooling retrofit to address water consumption and density issues. Utah drought concerns add urgency.
confidence_reason: Baxtel, Wikipedia, Grist, KUER confirm location, power, water use. Feb 2025 Army Corps EA confirms expansion plans. Immersion cooling not yet deployed here but NSA has tested it elsewhere.

source_1_evidence_snippet: Bluffdale, UT. 65MW constant usage. 4 data halls, 25,000 sq ft each, 18.75MW critical each. Completed 2014. $1.7B.
source_2_title: Environmental Assessment for the Utah Data Center Campus
source_2_evidence_snippet: Feb 2025 draft environmental assessment for NSA Utah Data Center campus — indicates potential expansion or upgrade activity.
notes: MAJOR RETROFIT OPPORTUNITY. Largest NSA facility. Extreme water consumption in drought-prone area. 2025 Army Corps EA signals expansion. NSA has independently proven immersion cooling (GRC ICEraQ, PUE 1.05). Immersion retrofit would eliminate water dependency and increase rack density.
expansion_details: Feb 2025 Army Corps environmental assessment indicates campus expansion/upgrade activity
pipeline_evidence_snippet: Draft EA filed Feb 2025 for NSA Utah Data Center campus.

facility_name: US Naval Research Laboratory — Two-Phase Immersion Cooling Demo (3M/SGI)
parent_company: US Department of Defense — US Navy

customer_type_if_known: US Navy — HPC research, ESTCP-funded demonstration
build_phase_if_known: Two-phase immersion cooling demo completed ~2016. ESTCP-funded. Met efficiency/GHG objectives but IT equipment failures noted.
cooling_type: Two-phase liquid immersion cooling — 3M Novec 649 Engineered Fluid. SGI IceX hardware (half-rack, 144 Sandybridge sockets, 40kW+). Dry cooler outside HPC center. Boiling on component surfaces for heat removal. ESTCP-funded demo met energy efficiency and GHG reduction objectives. However, IT equipment failures and costs noted as obstacles to commercialization.
opportunity_summary: NRL hosted ESTCP-funded two-phase immersion cooling demonstration with 3M Novec 649 and SGI IceX hardware. Met DoD energy efficiency and greenhouse gas reduction objectives. Partners: Lawrence Berkeley National Lab, APC/Schneider Electric. Obstacles identified: IT equipment failures in two-phase fluid. Root cause analysis recommended. Important early DoD immersion cooling research site.
confidence_reason: LBNL technical report (LBNL-1005666), OSTI.gov, DataCenterKnowledge confirm NRL demo. ESTCP funding confirmed. Equipment failures documented. Two-phase not yet viable at time of testing.
source_1_title: Immersion Cooling of Electronics in DoD Installations
source_1_evidence_snippet: Two-phase immersion cooling demo at NRL using 3M Novec 649. SGI IceX hardware. Met efficiency objectives but IT equipment failures noted. Root cause analysis recommended.
source_2_title: Immersion Cooling of Electronics in DoD Installations (Technical Report)
source_2_evidence_snippet: ESTCP-funded project. 3M Novec 649 two-phase immersion. NRL HPC center. Performance objectives met for energy and GHG. Equipment failure obstacle documented.
notes: DOD RESEARCH DEMO. Important early two-phase immersion cooling test in military context. ESTCP is DoD's environmental technology program. Results informed subsequent DoD cooling decisions. Two-phase viability issues may drive preference toward single-phase (GRC-style) solutions.
pipeline_evidence_snippet: ESTCP-funded demo completed ~2016 at NRL.

facility_name: Sandia National Laboratories — Submer Immersion Cooling HPC Test
parent_company: US Department of Energy — National Nuclear Security Administration
full_address: Sandia National Laboratories, Albuquerque, NM 87185
facility_type: National Lab / HPC Center (Nuclear Weapons Complex)
customer_type_if_known: DOE NNSA — High Performance Computing for nuclear weapons simulation
build_phase_if_known: Submer immersion cooling formal testing summer 2024. Case study expected fall 2024.
cooling_type: Single-phase liquid immersion cooling — Submer Technologies (Barcelona). Biodegradable, nontoxic, nonflammable, noncorrosive dielectric liquid. Whole racks submerged. Viscosity similar to cooking oil. 95% more efficient than traditional cooling. 1,400x better thermal conductor than air. Estimated 70% power reduction for compute-intensive HPC-AI clusters.
opportunity_summary: Sandia National Laboratories formally tested Submer immersion cooling in summer 2024 at its HPC center. Partnership through Adacen Inc. (Albuquerque). Submer provided fluid and equipment; Sandia provided cooling expertise. Estimated 70% power reduction for HPC-AI clusters. Evaluating hardware reliability and infrastructure cost. Case study expected. If positive, could influence DOE-wide adoption across national labs.
confidence_reason: Sandia LabNews (May 2024), InsideHPC (June 2024), DCD, HPCwire all confirm Submer immersion testing. Sandia press release is primary source. Adacen Inc. partnership confirmed.
source_1_title: Electrical circuits encased in fluid may reshape data-center design
source_1_evidence_snippet: Sandia HPC center formally tested Submer immersion cooling summer 2024. Whole computers placed in dielectric fluid. Partnership through Adacen Inc. 70% power reduction estimated.
source_2_title: Taking Liquid Cooling to Next Level: Sandia Researchers Say Direct Immersion May Cut Power Use by 70%
source_2_evidence_snippet: Sandia tested Submer commercial immersion cooling system. Biodegradable, nontoxic liquid. 95% more efficient. 1,400x better thermal conductor than air.
notes: DOE/NNSA NUCLEAR WEAPONS LAB. Testing immersion cooling for HPC-AI workloads. If case study is positive, implications for DOE-wide adoption (Oak Ridge, Argonne, LLNL, etc.). Submer is Barcelona-based; this is a US government adoption of European immersion technology.
expansion_details: Case study expected fall 2024. Potential DOE-wide adoption.
pipeline_evidence_snippet: Formal testing summer 2024. Case study expected fall 2024.
